{{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}}

| Badge                 = 
| Badge_size            = 200px

| Confederation         = [[Asian Football Confederation|AFC]] (Asia)
| Sub-confederation     = [[South Asian Football Federation|SAFF]] (South Asia)
| Coach                 = [[Abdullah Al Mutairi]]
| Captain               = [[Sujan Perera]]
| Most caps             = [[Channa Ediri Bandanage]] (66)
| Top scorer            = [[Kasun Jayasuriya]] (27)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/jayasuriya-intlg.html |title=Kasun Nadika Jayasuriya Weerarathne – Goals in International Matches |author=László Földesi |access-date=7 September 2018 |archive-date=5 September 2018 |website=[[RSSSF]] |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180905191627/http://www.rsssf.com/miscellaneous/jayasuriya-intlg.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
| Home Stadium          = [[Sugathadasa Stadium]]<br />[[Colombo Racecourse]]
| FIFA Trigramme        = SRI
| FIFA Rank             = {{FIFA World Rankings|SRI}}
| FIFA max              = 122
| FIFA max date         = August 1998
| FIFA min              = 207
| FIFA min date         = October 2022
| Elo Rank              = {{World Football Elo Ratings|Sri Lanka}}
| Elo max               = 105
| Elo max date          = 23 March 1952
| Elo min               = 225
| Elo min date          = September 2019
|pattern_la1            = 
|pattern_b1             = _srilanka24h
|pattern_ra1            = 
|pattern_sh1            = 
|pattern_so1            =

|body1                  = FFFFFF
|rightarm1              = FFFFFF
|shorts1                = FFFFFF
|socks1                 = FFFFFF

|pattern_b2             = _srilanka24a
|pattern_ra2            =

|leftarm2               = 0000AA
|body2                  = 0000AA
|rightarm2              = 0000AA
|shorts2                = 0000AA
|socks2                 = 0000AA
| First game            = {{fb|CEY|1948}} 0–1 {{fb-rt|IND}}<br />([[Colombo]], [[Dominion of Ceylon|Ceylon]]; 26 April 1949)
| Largest win           = {{fb|SRI}} 7–1 {{fb-rt|PAK}}<br />([[Taipei]], [[Taiwan]]; 4 April 2008)<br />{{fb|SRI}} 6–0 {{fb-rt|BHU}}<br />([[Dhaka]], [[Bangladesh]]; 6 December 2009)
| Largest loss          = 
{{fb|Ceylon|1951}} 1–12 {{fb-rt|GDR}}<br />([[Colombo]], [[Dominion of Ceylon|Ceylon]]; 12 January 1964)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://wildstat.com/p/7001/ch/all/team1/SRI/team2/GDR|title=Head to head stats Sri Lanka – GDR|website=WildStat|access-date=4 May 2018|archive-date=4 May 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180504225305/http://wildstat.com/p/7001/ch/all/team1/SRI/team2/GDR|url-status=live}}</ref>
| Regional name         = [[SAFF Championship]]
| Regional cup apps     = 13
| Regional cup first    = [[1993 SAARC Gold Cup|1993]]
| Regional cup best     = '''Champions''' ([[1995 SAARC Gold Cup|1995]])
| 2ndRegional name      = [[AFC Challenge Cup]]
| 2ndRegional cup apps  = 3
| 2ndRegional cup first = [[2006 AFC Challenge Cup|2006]]
| 2ndRegional cup best  = Runners-up ([[2006 AFC Challenge Cup|2006]])
}}

The '''Sri Lanka national football team''' ({{langx|si|ශ්‍රී ලංකා පාපන්දු කණ්ඩායම}} ''Shri Lanka Papandu Kandayama'', {{langx|ta|இலங்கை தேசிய கால்பந்து அணி}} ''Ilaṅkai Tēciya Kālpantu Aṇi'') represents [[Sri Lanka]] in [[Association football]] and is administered by [[Football Federation of Sri Lanka]], the governing body of football in Sri Lanka. They have been a member of [[FIFA]] since 1952 and a member of [[Asian Football Confederation|AFC]] since 1954. Sri Lanka's home stadium is the [[Sugathadasa Stadium]] in Colombo. The Sri Lankan team was known as the '''Ceylon national football team''' until 1972 when [[Ceylon]] was renamed Sri Lanka.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.sundaytimes.lk/130623/sports/sri-lankas-first-international-win-at-football-49901.html|title=Sri Lanka's first international win at football|publisher=The Sundaytimes Sri Lanka|date=23 June 2013|website=sundaytimes.lk|access-date=6 February 2017|archive-date=4 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304061513/http://www.sundaytimes.lk/130623/sports/sri-lankas-first-international-win-at-football-49901.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

A member of the [[Asian Football Confederation|AFC]], the team has yet to make their first appearance in [[FIFA World Cup]] or [[AFC Asian Cup]] finals. They have been South Asian champions once, in 1995. As is true elsewhere on the sub-continent, top-level football in Sri Lanka stands somewhat in the shadow of the country's [[Cricket]] team. However, the side did reach the second qualification stage for the [[2006 FIFA World Cup|2006 World Cup]]. In the same year, they became the runners-up in the [[2006 AFC Challenge Cup|2006 Challenge Cup]].

In the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|qualification round]] of the [[2018 FIFA World Cup|2018 World Cup]], Sri Lanka lost both matches against [[Bhutan national football team|Bhutan]] and failed to qualify for the next round. However, Sri Lanka national football team had managed to qualify for the semi-finals of the [[2015 SAFF Championship]].

== History ==

=== Early history ===
In January 1933, an All-Ceylon side lost 0–1 against the touring Indian football team. Though it was an All-Bengal team organised by the [[Indian Football Association|IFA]], it was a de facto [[India national football team|Indian national football team]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Times of Ceylon 1933.02.01 — South Asian Newspapers |url=https://gpa.eastview.com/crl/san/?a=d&d=tocy19330201-01.1.5 |access-date=2025-12-04 |website=gpa.eastview.com}}</ref><ref name="thehardtackle.com">{{cite web |date=20 August 2011 |title=Legends of Indian Football: Gostha Pal |url=http://www.thehardtackle.com/2011/legends-of-indian-football-gostha-pal/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180820140949/http://www.thehardtackle.com/2011/legends-of-indian-football-gostha-pal/ |archive-date=20 August 2018 |access-date=2018-08-20}}</ref> 

In 1939, the [[Football Federation of Sri Lanka|Ceylon Football Association]] was formed.<ref>https://archives1.dailynews.lk/2013/07/24/sports/sri-lankan-football-and-its-origin?page=53 {{Bare URL inline|date=July 2026}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Great Footballers of the past {{!}} The Sundaytimes Sri Lanka |url=http://www.sundaytimes.lk/140601/sports/great-footballers-of-the-past-100957.html |access-date=2025-12-20}}</ref>
[[File:Ceylon and India national football team group picture, 1949.png|left|thumb|Ceylon and [[India national football team|India]] national football teams pictured together during their friendly match in 1949]]
In 1946 and 1948, Ceylon played against touring Indian sides.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Times of Ceylon 1946.01.04 — South Asian Newspapers |url=https://gpa.eastview.com/crl/san/?a=d&d=tocy19460104-01.1.6 |access-date=2025-12-05 |website=gpa.eastview.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Times of Ceylon 1948.02.19 — South Asian Newspapers |url=https://gpa.eastview.com/crl/san/?a=d&d=tocy19480219-01.1.9 |access-date=2025-12-04 |website=gpa.eastview.com}}</ref> In April 1949, the team played two test matches against the touring Indian national football team, losing 0–1 and 1–6 respectively.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Times of Ceylon 1949.04.27 — South Asian Newspapers |url=https://gpa.eastview.com/crl/san/?a=d&d=tocy19490427-01.1.9 |access-date=2025-12-04 |website=gpa.eastview.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Times of Ceylon 1949.04.30 — South Asian Newspapers |url=https://gpa.eastview.com/crl/san/?a=d&d=tocy19490430-01.1.9 |access-date=2025-12-04 |website=gpa.eastview.com}}</ref> The same year, Ceylon played two matches against the touring Pakistani team [[Jinnah Gymkhana FC|Jinnah Gymkhana]], losing 0–4 and 0–1.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Times of Ceylon 1949.11.30 — South Asian Newspapers |url=https://gpa.eastview.com/crl/san/?a=d&d=tocy19491130-01.1.11 |access-date=2025-12-04 |website=gpa.eastview.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Times of Ceylon 1949.12.03 — South Asian Newspapers |url=https://gpa.eastview.com/crl/san/?a=d&d=tocy19491203-01.1.9 |access-date=2025-12-05 |website=gpa.eastview.com}}</ref>

=== FIFA recognition (1950s) ===
In 1952, Ceylon became a member of [[FIFA]]. The team participated in the [[1952 Asian Quadrangular Football Tournament]], held in Colombo.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=Morrison |first=Neil |date=1999 |title=Asian Quadrangular Tournament (Colombo Cup) 1952–1955: 1953 (Rangoon, Burma) |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesb/brindies-quad.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220927000411/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesb/brindies-quad.html |archive-date=27 September 2022 |website=[[RSSSF]]}}</ref> Later on, the team also participated in the [[1953 Asian Quadrangular Football Tournament|1953]], [[1954 Asian Quadrangular Football Tournament|1954]] and [[1955 Asian Quadrangular Football Tournament|1955]] editions of the tournament.<ref name=":0" />

In 1956, the team played two test matches against the touring [[Pakistan national football team|Pakistan national team]], losing and drawing once.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Singapore Standard, 4 September 1956, Page 13 |url=https://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/digitised/article/singstandard19560904-1.2.163 |website=eresources.nlb.gov.sg}}</ref> The next year, Ceylon played against the touring Soviet team [[Neftçi PFK|Neftyanik]].<ref>{{Cite web |title= |url=https://www.pressreader.com/sri-lanka/sunday-times-sri-lanka/20120422/282402691380175?srsltid=AfmBOoqycxnBKynf5bkiZfwGbLph5Ml5mxXYPeGXCeddOH_mXP5HJDke |access-date=2025-12-20 |via=PressReader}}</ref>

In 1958, the team toured the [[Far East|far east]] under the captainship of [[Tom Ossen]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Sri Lanka's international approach in football |url=http://www.dailynews.lk/2020/07/02/sports/222053/sri-lanka’s-international-approach-football?page=16 |website=dailynews.lk}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Kandy Football needs a wake up call |url=http://www.sundaytimes.lk/170917/sports/kandy-football-needs-a-wake-up-call-259766.html |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=The Sunday Times Sri Lanka}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=National Teams Touring China |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesc/chinatours-natteams.html |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.rsssf.org}}</ref>

=== 1960s ===
In January 1960, Ceylon played two test matches against touring soviet club [[FC Zenit Saint Petersburg|Zenit Leningrad]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=История «Зенита» - 21.01.1960 – Сборная Цейлона – «Зенит» |url=https://www.zenit-history.ru/kalendar/621-etot-den-v-istorii-zenita/yanvar/12329-21-01-1960-sbornaya-tsejlona-zenit |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.zenit-history.ru}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=История «Зенита» - 24.01.1960 – последний гол Александра Иванова за «Зенит» в международных матчах |url=https://www.zenit-history.ru/matchi/621-etot-den-v-istorii-zenita/yanvar/9411-24-01-1960-poslednij-gol-aleksandra-ivanova-v-mezhdunarodnykh-matchakh-za-zenit |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.zenit-history.ru}}</ref> The next year the team played test matches against touring Soviet club [[BC Sparta Praha|Spartak Praha Sokolovo]] and Brazilian club [[Madureira Esporte Clube|Madureira]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=TJ Spartak Praha Sokolovo (Czechoslovakia) tour of Asia 1961 |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/spsokolovo-asia61.html |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.rsssf.org}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Madureira EC (Brazil) tour of Asia 1961 |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesm/madureira-trip61.html |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.rsssf.org}}</ref> Ceylon began participating in the Southern Pentangular Tournament, a regional football competition held among Ceylon and leading Indian state teams during the 1960s, later reduced to a quadrangular format in 1970. Ceylon took part in all editions held in 1961 (Bangalore), 1962 (Madras), 1963 (Colombo), 1964 (Andhra Pradesh), 1967 (Colombo), and 1970 (Colombo). The final edition in 1970, played at the [[Sugathadasa Stadium]], saw Ceylon emerge as unbeaten champions, marking one of the national team's earliest major international achievements.<ref>{{Cite web |title=When Ceylon became unbeaten football champions {{!}} The Sundaytimes Sri Lanka |url=http://www.sundaytimes.lk/130915/sports/when-ceylon-became-unbeaten-football-champions-62365.html |access-date=2025-12-20}}</ref>

In 1963, Ceylon participated in the [[Football at the 1964 Summer Olympics – Men's qualification|1964 Summer Olympics qualifiers]], losing against [[India national football team|India]] in the home and away fixtures.<ref>{{Cite web |title=An unforgettable soccer encounter {{!}} The Sunday Times Sri Lanka |url=http://www.sundaytimes.lk/141116/sports/an-unforgettable-soccer-encounter-2-128195.html |access-date=2025-12-20}}</ref> The next year, the team played against the touring [[East Germany national football team|East Germany]] team, recording a 1–12 defeat.<ref>{{Cite book |title=Die Neue Fussball Woche |publication-date=21 January 1964 |pages=8–9 |language=German}}</ref> The same year, the team also played two tests against touring [[Indonesia national football team|Indonesia]].

On 28 February 1965, Ceylon secured their first international victory on home soil, defeating [[Pakistan national football team|Pakistan]] 3–1 in a friendly at the [[Sugathadasa Stadium]]. The second friendly match was tied 0–0.<ref name="1965win">{{Cite web |title=Sri Lankan's famous win in 1965 |url=https://www.sundaytimes.lk/130707/sports/sri-lankans-famous-win-in-1965-51819.html |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230530080415/https://www.sundaytimes.lk/130707/sports/sri-lankans-famous-win-in-1965-51819.html |archive-date=30 May 2023 |access-date=2024-08-15 |publisher=The Sundaytimes Sri Lanka}}</ref> The same year, the team toured Indonesia, and also played against German side [[1. FC Nürnberg]] in home venue.<ref>{{Cite web |title=1. FC Nürnberg tour of Asia 1965 |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesn/nuernberg68asiatour.html |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.rsssf.org}}</ref> In 1967, the team also played against touring American side [[Dallas Tornado]],<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.nasljerseys.com/Misc/Tornado%2067-68%20World%20Tour2.htm |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.nasljerseys.com}}</ref> and Soviet team [[FK Žalgiris]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kalinauskas |first=Gediminas |title=Vilniaus "Žalgiris". // Vilnius, "Diemedis" |year=2000 |isbn=9986230829 |pages=184–185}}</ref>

In 1966, Ceylon played two friendly matches against touring [[Myanmar national football team|Burma]], losing 0–3 and 1–3.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Straits Times, 10 April 1966, Page 16 |url=https://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/digitised/article/straitstimes19660410-1.2.68 |website=eresources.nlb.gov.sg}}</ref>

Ceylon participated in the [[Football at the 1968 Summer Olympics – Men's qualification|1968 Summer Olympics qualifiers]], losing against [[Israel national football team|Israel]] in both home and away fixtures. Later on, the team embarked on a tour in Israel and Germany, playing against local sides including clubs from [[Übach-Palenberg|Palenberg]], [[Essen]] and [[Hamburg]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Behind the net saga of that European tour {{!}} The Sundaytimes Sri Lanka |url=http://www.sundaytimes.lk/130721/sports/behind-the-net-saga-of-that-european-tour-53826.html |access-date=2025-12-20}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Legends of Sri Lanka soccer {{!}} The Sundaytimes Sri Lanka |url=http://www.sundaytimes.lk/140615/sports/legends-of-sri-lanka-soccer-103436.html |access-date=2025-12-20}}</ref>

=== 1970s ===
In 1971, the team participated in the [[1972 AFC Asian Cup qualification]], losing all matches against [[Iraq national football team|Iraq]], [[Jordan national football team|Jordan]] and [[Bahrain national football team|Bahrain]]. The next year, the team again failed to advance at the [[Football at the 1972 Summer Olympics – Men's Asian Qualifiers|1972 Summer Olympics Qualifiers]], losing all matches. Later on, the team also participated in the [[1972 Jakarta Anniversary Tournament]] and the [[1972 Merdeka Tournament]]. The same year, the team also played friendlies against [[Singapore national football team|Singapore]] in away venue and [[China national football team|China]] in home.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Straits Times, 24 June 1972, Page 25 |url=https://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/digitised/article/straitstimes19720624-1.2.106.1 |website=eresources.nlb.gov.sg}}</ref> Soviet club [[FC Dinamo Tbilisi|Dinamo Tbilisi]], who toured Ceylon also played test matches against the Ceylon national side.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Шри-Ланка - «Динамо» Тбилиси - 0:7 29.11.1972 - Футбольные протоколы, матчи - footballfakts.ru |url=https://footballfakts.ru/match/1114270-shrilankadinamotbilisi07 |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=FootballFakts.ru {{!}} Футбольная статистика и история |language=ru}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Шри-Ланка - «Динамо» Тбилиси - 0:4 02.12.1972 - Футбольные протоколы, матчи - footballfakts.ru |url=https://footballfakts.ru/match/1114271-shrilankadinamotbilisi04 |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=FootballFakts.ru {{!}} Футбольная статистика и история |language=ru}}</ref>

In 1979, Sri Lanka featured in the [[1980 AFC Asian Cup qualification]], [[1979 President's Cup Football Tournament]] and the [[1979 King's Cup]] in Thailand. The team also played against Swedish club Kronängs IF; the tour was organised by Kronängs player Norman Alfonso, a former player of Saunders club of Sri Lanka.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kronängs IF (Sweden) tour of Sri Lanka 1979 |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesk/kronang-sri79.html |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.rsssf.org}}</ref>

=== 1980s ===
At the [[Football at the 1980 Summer Olympics – Men's Asian Qualifiers|1980 Summer Olympics Qualifiers]], Sri Lanka failed to advance after losing against [[Singapore national football team|Singapore]], [[North Korea national football team|North Korea]], [[India national football team|India]], [[China national football team|China]] and [[Iran national football team|Iran]].

In 1983, the team played friendlies against [[Maldives national football team|Maldives]] and [[Singapore national football team|Singapore]]. In 1984, after playing a friendly against the touring Maldives, the team participated in the [[1984 AFC Asian Cup qualification]]. Sri Lanka participated in the [[1986 Quaid-e-Azam International Tournament]] held in Islamabad, ending in the fourth position.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Quaid-E-Azam International Cup (Pakistan) |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesq/quaid-e-azam.html |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.rsssf.org}}</ref>

At the [[Football at the 1989 South Asian Games|1989 South Asian Games]], the team failed to advance past the group stage.<ref>{{Cite web |title=4th South Asian Federation Games 1989 (Islamabad, Pakistan) |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/safg89.html |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.rsssf.org}}</ref>

=== Regional success (1990s) ===
The team participated at the [[Football at the 1991 South Asian Games|1991 South Asian Games]], again ending unsuccessful.

In 1993, Sri Lanka participated for the first time at the [[1994 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|1994 FIFA World Cup qualification]], losing all the matches. The same year, the team participated in the [[1993 SAARC Gold Cup]] and the [[Football at the 1993 South Asian Games|1993 South Asian Games]], achieving bronze, after defeating Maldives by 3–1 in the third place match.<ref>{{Cite web |title=6th South Asian Federation Games 1993 (Bangladesh) |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/safg93.html |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.rsssf.org}}</ref>[[File:Sampath Perera and Qazi Ashfaq before the match between Sri Lanka and Pakistan, 1997 Gold Cup.jpg|thumb|Sri Lanka and [[Pakistan national football team|Pakistan]] during the Third-place match at the [[1997 SAFF Gold Cup]]]]

In 1995, Sri Lanka made history winning the [[1995 SAARC Gold Cup]], after defeating India in the final by 1–0.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-12-05 |title=New football column: CORNER KICK BY MARADONA - Revisiting Sri Lanka's 1995 SAARC Gold Cup win |url=https://themorning.lk//articles/177656 |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=Latest in the News Sphere {{!}} The Morning}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=FSL honours 1995 SAARC Gold Cup-winning football team |url=https://www.dailymirror.lk/print/sports/FSL-honours-1995-SAARC-Gold-Cup-winning-football-team/244-229687 |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.dailymirror.lk |language=English}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=admin |first=eLanka |date=2021-05-25 |title=When Sri Lanka won the SAARC Gold Cup in 1995-by Hafiz Marikar |url=https://www.elanka.com.au/when-sri-lanka-won-the-saarc-gold-cup-in-1995-by-hafiz-marikar/ |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=eLanka |language=en-US}}</ref> At the [[Football at the 1995 South Asian Games|1995 South Asian Games]], the team achieved bronze, after defeating [[Nepal national football team|Nepal]] in the penalty shootout after a goalless draw.<ref>{{Cite web |title=7th South Asian Federation Games 1995 |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/safg95.html |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.rsssf.org}}</ref> The team also played at the [[Burma Tournament 1995]].

In 1996, the team ended unsuccessful at the [[1996 AFC Asian Cup qualification]] and the [[1998 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round|FIFA World Cup qualification]].

At the [[1997 SAFF Gold Cup]], Sri Lanka was ousted in the semifinal after losing against eventual finalists [[Maldives]]. The next year the team played at the Bristol Freedom Cup organised by the Sri Lanka Football Federation. The team eventually lost in the final against India B.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Bristol Freedom Cup |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesb/bristol-free98.html |access-date=2025-12-20 |website=www.rsssf.org}}</ref>

In 1999, Sri Lanka participated in the [[1999 SAFF Gold Cup]], [[Football at the 1999 South Asian Games|1999 South Asian Games]] and the [[2000 AFC Asian Cup qualification]].

=== 2000s ===
{{Empty section|date=March 2026}}

=== Renaissance of Sri Lanka football (2015–2019) ===

In 2015, Sri Lanka played for the first time in the [[Bangabandhu Cup]]. This tournament was organised by [[Bangladesh Football Federation]]. The national U-23 teams of [[Thailand]], [[Bahrain]], [[Malaysia]] and [[Singapore]] participated in this tournament. The national football teams of [[Bangladesh]] and Sri Lanka also competed in this tournament.

Sri Lanka was placed in the group with Malaysia and Bangladesh. In the first game Sri Lanka played against the Malaysian team. Malaysia won the match by 2–0. The second match was played against the host Bangladesh. Sri Lanka lost the game 1–0. Sri Lanka failed to score a goal in this tournament. After a six years of poor performance in the international football field Sri Lanka football team managed to qualify for the Semi Final of [[2015 SAFF Championship]]. The poor performance continued in the [[2016 AFC Solidarity Cup|Solidarity Cup]] as well. They had another shock defeat this time by the hands of [[Mongolia national football team|Mongolia]] the lowest ranked team in Asia. As a result of this defeat Sri Lanka eliminated in the group stage of the tournament.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.thepapare.com/live-mongolia-vs-sri-lanka-afc-solidarity-cup-2016/|title=Mongolian penalties down Sri Lanka|last=AFC|date=6 November 2016|work=thepapare.com|access-date=6 February 2017|archive-date=24 April 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170424230007/http://www.thepapare.com/live-mongolia-vs-sri-lanka-afc-solidarity-cup-2016/|url-status=live}}</ref>

In July 2018, Sri Lanka faced Lithuania B, manahging a respectable 0–0 draw.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Edirisinghe |first=Pulasthi |date=2018-07-08 |title=Commendable Sri Lanka draw against Lithuania |url=https://www.thepapare.com/match-report-sri-lanka-lithuania-football-friendly-8th-july-2018/ |access-date=2026-03-03 |website=ThePapare |language=en-US}}</ref> During [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2022 World Cup campaign]] however, Sri Lanka suffered another poor performance as the team fell 0–1 in [[Zhuhai]] to Macau. Macau was subsequently disqualified, as the team refused to travel to Sri Lanka in the aftermath of [[2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings]],<ref>{{cite web| url = https://www.thetelegram.com/sports/world-cup-qualifier-in-sri-lanka-canceled-after-macau-refuse-to-travel-320518/| title = World Cup qualifier in Sri Lanka canceled after Macau refuse to travel {{!}} SaltWire| access-date = 2 July 2019| archive-date = 2 July 2019| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20190702163142/https://www.thetelegram.com/sports/world-cup-qualifier-in-sri-lanka-canceled-after-macau-refuse-to-travel-320518/| url-status = live}}</ref> Sri Lanka was awarded a 3–0 win in response, thus qualified to the second round of the World Cup for the first time since 2006 campaign.<ref>{{Cite web|url = https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/soccer/2019/06/29/sri-lanka-welcomes-fifa-ruling-macau-out-of-wcup-qualifying/39638659/|title = Sri Lanka welcomes FIFA ruling Macau out of WCup qualifying|website = [[USA Today]]|access-date = 2 July 2019|archive-date = 2 July 2019|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20190702163140/https://www.usatoday.com/story/sports/soccer/2019/06/29/sri-lanka-welcomes-fifa-ruling-macau-out-of-wcup-qualifying/39638659/|url-status = live}}</ref>

=== Amir Alagic era (2020–2022) ===
{{football squad on pitch|align=right|clear=none
| GK = [[Weerasinghe Sujan Perera|'''Sujan (C)''']]
| RB = '''M. Aakib'''
| LCB = '''C. Dilshan'''
| RCB = [[Harsha Fernando|'''H. Fernando''']]
| LB = [[Chalana Chameera|'''C. Chameera''']]
| DM = [[Asikur Rahuman|'''Asikur''']]
| CM = '''Rathnayake'''
| LM = '''Basith'''
| RM = '''Shazny'''
| RCF = '''[[Ahmed Waseem Razeek|Razeek]]'''
| LCF = [[Marvin Hamilton|'''Hamilton''']]
| caption = Sri Lanka's starting lineup for the [[2021 Four Nations Football Tournament|Four Nations Football Tournament 2021 final]]
}}
In the [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC second round|second round]], Sri Lanka was unlucky to be drawn with four [[2019 AFC Asian Cup]] participants, [[South Korea national football team|South Korea]], [[North Korea national football team|North Korea]], [[Lebanon national football team|Lebanon]] and [[Turkmenistan national football team|Turkmenistan]]. As predicted, Sri Lanka proved too weak for the group, losing all matches without scoring a single goal as for the end of 2019, and was eliminated from World Cup contention. Sri Lanka had improved since then, and participated in the [[2020 Bangabandhu Cup]], but the team still finished bottom with two defeats and no goal.

In February 2020, the [[Football Federation of Sri Lanka]] announced the appointment of Bosnian-Australian specialist [[Amir Alagić]] as head coach of the national team.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://turkmenportal.com/blog/25433/sbornaya-shrilanki-po-futbolu-naznachila-novogo-glavnogo-trenera |title=Сборная Шри-Ланки по футболу назначила нового главного тренера |date=24 February 2020 |access-date=24 February 2020 |archive-date=24 February 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200224174019/https://turkmenportal.com/blog/25433/sbornaya-shrilanki-po-futbolu-naznachila-novogo-glavnogo-trenera |url-status=live }}</ref> Sri Lanka then travelled [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC second round#Group H|to South Korea to finish their two remaining games against Lebanon and South Korea, losing both]], yet optimism rose when Sri Lanka demonstrated an outstanding performance against Lebanon, scoring two goals and only lost by one goal margin, which was also the country's first-ever goals in the [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC second round#LBN v SRI|qualification]]. Alagić resigned as coach of Sri Lanka after the qualification, as Sri Lanka, rated as the weakest team in the group, were eliminated without scoring a point. He was replaced by Scottish manager, [[Andy Morrison]].

=== FIFA suspension and Participation in 2026 FIFA World Cup qualifying (2023) ===
On 22 January 2023, [[FIFA]] announced the suspension of the FFSL from 21 January 2023 until further notice due to the government interference in football, but was provisionally included in the draw, pending a resolution. Therefore, all teams/clubs affiliated with the FFSL are no longer entitled to take part in international competitions.<ref name="Sri Lanka">{{cite web|url=https://digitalhub.fifa.com/m/5d07bb14cb642349/original/Circular-1831_Suspension-FFSL_EN.pdf|title=Suspension of the Football Federation of Sri Lanka|publisher=International Association Football Federation|date=22 January 2023}}.</ref> However, after the issue was settled, the ban was lifted allowing Sri Lanka to take part in the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)|2026 FIFA World Cup qualification]] under a strict condition: FFSL must conduct a new election ten days before the fixture against [[Yemen national football team|Yemen]]; failure to do so would result in automatic disqualification of the team.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.newswire.lk/2023/07/26/sri-lanka-football-ban-new-update-from-fifa/ | title=Sri Lanka Football ban : New update from FIFA | date=26 July 2023 }}</ref> The suspension was lifted on 29 August 2023.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fifa.com/about-fifa/associations/news/bureau-of-the-fifa-council-lifts-suspension-on-football-federation-of-sri-lanka|title=Bureau of the FIFA Council lifts suspension on Football Federation of Sri Lanka|publisher=[[FIFA]]|date=28 August 2023|access-date=20 September 2023}}</ref>

Sri Lanka then participated in the first round of the [[2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC first round|2026 FIFA World Cup qualification]] facing against [[Yemen national football team|Yemen]] on 12 October 2023 but lost 4–1 on aggregate in the process. In September 2024, Sri Lanka then played in the [[2027 AFC Asian Cup qualification – play-off round|2027 AFC Asian Cup qualification play-off round]] against [[Cambodia national football team|Cambodia]] in which the aggregate was tied on 2–2 where [[Claudio Kammerknecht]] scored in the 120+2th minute in extra time to send the game to penalties shoot-out. Sri Lanka came out victorious thus qualifying to the [[2027 AFC Asian Cup qualification – third round|third round]].

=== 2027 AFC Asian Cup Qualification (2024–) ===
The Sri Lanka national football team began their [[2027 AFC Asian Cup qualification]] campaign by integrating players of Sri Lankan origin from overseas, including Claudio Kammerknecht and Marvin Hamilton. Their contributions were pivotal in the team's penalty shoot-out victory over Cambodia in the play-off round, securing a place in the third round.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.themorning.lk/articles/FBi5uadXZs4liu2SNbzX|title=FFSL charts future path after Asian Cup breakthrough|publisher=The Morning|date=September 2024}}</ref>

In September 2024, Kuwaiti head coach [[Abdullah Al Mutairi]] was appointed to prepare the team for the next stage of qualification.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.sundaytimes.lk/240929/sports/sri-lanka-secure-al-mutairi-as-head-coach-572723.html|title=Sri Lanka secure Al-Mutairi as head coach|publisher=The Sunday Times|date=29 September 2024}}</ref> Sri Lanka is scheduled to play six international friendlies in 2025 to build match fitness and test tactical adjustments ahead of the third round.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.dailymirror.lk/print/sports/Sri-Lanka-to-play-six-internationals-in-2025/244-297958|title=Sri Lanka to play six internationals in 2025|publisher=Daily Mirror|date=2024}}</ref> The third round of qualification will take place in 2025, where Sri Lanka has been drawn into Group D alongside [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]], [[Turkmenistan national football team|Turkmenistan]], and [[Chinese Taipei national football team|Chinese Taipei]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/afc_asian_cup/news/hopefuls_discover_saudi_arabia_2027_qualifiers_final_round_challengers_1.html|title=Hopefuls discover Saudi Arabia 2027 Qualifiers Final Round challengers|publisher=The Asian Football Confederation|date=8 December 2024}}</ref> On 5 June 2025, Sri Lanka won Chinese Taipei 3–1 on home soil.

==Team image==
=== Kit ===
The team's crest features a blue and gold colour scheme, with a yellow lion holding a sword on a deep blue background. White is used for the text and border. These colours are reflected in the kits.

The team currently wears a predominantly white home kit and a blue away kit, introduced in 2023.<ref>{{cite web |title=Sri Lanka Kit History |url=https://www.footballkitarchive.com/sri-lanka-kits/ |website=Football Kit Archive |access-date=4 April 2026}}</ref> The home kit is primarily white, with a blue and yellow dotted gradient pattern across the chest and lower sections, complemented by blue detailing and yellow-and-blue side panels, and is paired with white shorts and socks.<ref>{{cite web |title=Sri Lanka 2023 Home Kit |url=https://www.footballkitarchive.com/sri-lanka-2023-home-kit/243302/ |website=Football Kit Archive |access-date=4 April 2026}}</ref> The away kit is predominantly blue with a lighter dotted pattern, and is paired with blue shorts and socks; it also features white names and numbers with subtle yellow accents.<ref>{{cite web |title=Sri Lanka 2023 Away Kit |url=https://www.footballkitarchive.com/sri-lanka-2023-away-kit/243301/ |website=Football Kit Archive |access-date=4 April 2026}}</ref>

Traditionally, the team wore gold and blue as their primary home colours, while white with gold elements was used for the away kit.

=== Home stadiums ===
==== Colombo Racecourse ====
The [[Colombo Racecourse]], located in Colombo, serves as the current home venue of the Sri Lanka national football team. Originally opened in 1893 as a horse racing track, the venue was repurposed as an airfield by the [[Royal Air Force]] during [[World War II]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Colombo's colonial sporting landmark and its colourful past |url=https://www.themorning.lk/articles/186023 |website=The Morning |date=25 January 2022 |access-date=10 June 2025}}</ref> In the early 2010s, the ground underwent significant renovations to convert it into a modern football and multi-sport stadium.<ref>{{cite web |title=Racecourse gets facelift |url=https://www.dailymirror.lk/print/caption-story/racecourse-gets-facelift/110-22855 |website=Daily Mirror |date=28 January 2013 |access-date=10 June 2025}}</ref>

Following a hiatus of more than three years in international football matches in Sri Lanka, the Colombo Racecourse hosted its first senior international match on 8 July 2018, a friendly between Sri Lanka and Lithuania B, which ended in a 0–0 draw.

As of 2025, the stadium has served as the national team's primary home ground, replacing the Sugathadasa Stadium in this role since 2015. It has a seating capacity of approximately 10,000 and includes facilities suitable for international competition.

==== Sugathadasa Stadium ====
[[File:Sugatadasa Stadium.jpg|thumb|right|Sugathadasa Stadium]]
[[Sugathadasa Stadium]] is the former athletic stadium in [[Sri Lanka]]. It was established in 1972 and has a capacity of 28,000.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://int.soccerway.com/national/sri-lanka/champions-league/20122013/regular-season/r21175/venues/?ICID=PL_3N_05|title=Stadions – Champions League – Sri Lanka – Resultaten, programma's, standen en nieuws – Soccerway|work=soccerway.com|access-date=6 February 2017|archive-date=31 January 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190131000915/https://int.soccerway.com/national/sri-lanka/champions-league/20122013/regular-season/r21175/venues/?ICID=PL_3N_05|url-status=live}}</ref> The stadium is mostly used for athletics and football. The 1995 and 2008 SAFF Championship tournaments were held in this stadium. This is the home stadium of Sri Lanka National Football Team. Sri Lanka won their first major football tournament in his stadium. It was defeating India in the Final of 1995 SAFF Championship.

All the [[FIFA World Cup]] qualification matches of Sri Lankan team prior to 2016 played in this ground. Major football tournaments that played in here were the AFC President's Cup and AFC Challenge Cup.

The last international match played at Sugathadasa took place on 12 March 2015, against Bhutan in a 2018 FIFA World Cup qualifying fixture.<ref>{{cite web |title=Sri Lanka vs Bhutan, 12 March 2015 |url=https://www.national-football-teams.com/matches/report/12665/Sri_Lanka_Bhutan.html |website=National Football Teams |access-date=10 June 2025}}</ref>

==== Kalutara Stadium ====
The [[Kalutara Stadium]], also known as the '''Vernon Fernando Stadium''' or locally as the '''Kalutara Park Ground''', is a multi-purpose venue in Kalutara with a capacity of around 15,000. It is primarily used for domestic club football. The last recorded international match played here was a friendly between Sri Lanka and Pakistan on 25 March 2002.<ref>{{cite web |title=Sri Lanka vs Pakistan, 25 March 2002 |url=https://www.national-football-teams.com/matches/report/15591/Sri_Lanka_Pakistan.html |website=National Football Teams |access-date=10 June 2025}}</ref>

=== Coaching history ===

{{Div col|colwidth=17em}}
* {{flagicon|SRI}} Neville Abeyagunawardena (1952–1968)<ref name=":coaches" /><ref>{{Cite web|title=Asian Quadrangular Tournament (Colombo Cup) 1952-1955|url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesb/brindies-quad.html|website=www.rsssf.org|access-date=2025-12-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://eu-football.info/_match.php?id=30012|website=eu-football.info|access-date=2025-12-01}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Behind the net saga of that European tour {{!}} The Sundaytimes Sri Lanka|url=http://www.sundaytimes.lk/130721/sports/behind-the-net-saga-of-that-european-tour-53826.html|access-date=2025-12-01}}</ref>
* {{flagicon|SRI}} V. Nadarajah (1969)<ref name=":coaches">{{Cite web|title=Lest we forget - a salute to our football coaches|url=https://www.sundaytimes.lk/120610/Sports/spt05.html|website=www.sundaytimes.lk|access-date=2025-12-01}}</ref>
* {{flagicon|SRI}} Lawrence Fernando (1970–1975)<ref name=":coaches" />
* {{flagicon|SRI}} [[Pathiniya Durage Sirisena|P. D. Sirisena]] (1976–1990)<ref>{{Cite web|first=Naushad|last=Amit|url=http://www.sundaytimes.lk/171119/sports/the-football-master-now-only-on-a-liquid-diet-269224.html|title=The football master now only on a liquid diet|date=19 November 2017|access-date=30 May 2024|publisher=The Sunday Times}}</ref>
* {{flagicon|SRI}} Neville Dias (1990)
* {{flagicon|GER}} [[Burkhard Pape]] (1991–1992)
* {{flagicon|BRA}} Jorge Ferreira (1993–1995)
* {{flagicon|SRI}} [[Pathiniya Durage Sirisena|P. D. Sirisena]] (1996)
* ''Unknown'' (1997–1998)
* {{flagicon|SRI}} [[Subhani Hassimdeen]] (1999)
* {{flagicon|MAS}} [[M. Karathu]] (1999–2000)
* {{flagicon|BRA}} Marcos Ferreira (2000–2004)
* {{flagicon|SRI}} [[Sampath Perera]] (2004–2006)
* {{flagicon|KOR|1997}} [[Jang Jung]] (2007–2008)
* {{flagicon|SRI}} [[Sampath Perera]] (2009)
* {{flagicon|SRI}} [[Mohamed Amanulla]] (2009–2010)
* {{flagicon|KOR|1997}} [[Jang Jung]] (2010–2011)
* {{flagicon|SRI}} [[Sampath Perera]] (2011–2013)
* {{flagicon|BRA}} [[Claudio Roberto]] (2013–2014)
* {{flagicon|SER}} [[Nikola Kavazović]] (2014–2015)
* {{flagicon|SRI}} [[Sampath Perera]] (2015–2016)
* {{flagicon|SRI}} [[Dudley Steinwall]] (2016–2018)
* {{flagicon|SRI}} [[Pakir Ali|Nizam Pakeer Ali]] (2018–2020)
* {{flagicon|BIH}} {{flagicon|AUS}} [[Amir Alagić]] (2020–2022)
* {{flagicon|SRI}} [[Mohamed Hassan Roomy]] (2022)
* {{flagicon|SCO}} [[Andy Morrison]] (2022–2024)<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.thepapare.com/andrew-morrison-keith-stevens-foreign-coaches-arrives-football-sri-lanka-2022/|title=Andrew Morrison and Keith Stevens as foreign coaches arrive in Sri Lanka|date=2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|last1=Today |first1=Ceylon |url=https://ceylontoday.lk/2023/11/25/ffsl-extends-andy-morrisons-contract-by-two-years/|title=FFSL extends Andy Morrison's contract by two years|work=Ceylon Today |date=November 25, 2023}}</ref>
* {{flagicon|KUW}} [[Abdullah Al Mutairi]] (2024–present)
{{div col end}}

==Players==
===Current squad===
The following 24 players were called up for the [[2027 AFC Asian Cup qualification – third round|2027 AFC Asian Cup qualifiers]] against [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]] on 10–19 November 2025.

''Caps and goals are correct as of 18 November 2025 after the game against [[Thailand national football team|Thailand]].''

{{nat fs g start}}
{{nat fs g player|no=1|pos=GK|name=[[Weerasinghe Sujan Perera|Sujan Perera]]|other=[[Captain (association football)|captain]]|age={{Birth date and age|1992|7|18|df=y}}|caps=62|goals=0|club=[[Fortis FC]]|clubnat=BAN}}

{{nat fs end}}

===Recent call-ups===
The following players have been called up for the team within the last 12 months and are still available for selection.
<!--Sorted by position, date of birth, caps, goals and most recent call-up.-->
{{nat fs r start}}
{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=DF|name=Manaram Perera|age={{Birth date and age|1998|6|23|df=y}}|caps=8|goals=0|club=[[Navy Sea Hawks FC|Navy Sea Hawks]]|clubnat=SRI|latest=v. {{fb|YEM}}, 16 November 2024}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Asikur Rahuman]]|age={{Birth date and age|1993|12|31|df=y}}|caps=29|goals=1|club=[[Defenders Football Club|Defenders]]|clubnat=SRI|latest=v. {{fb|BRU}}, 8 June 2024}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=DF|name=Steven Sacayaradjy|age={{Birth date and age|1997|1|22|df=y}}|caps=9|goals=0|club=Sud FC|clubnat=FRA|latest=v. {{fb|MDV}}, 6 September 2025}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Jack Hingert]]|age={{Birth date and age|1990|9|26|df=y}}|caps=10|goals=1|club=[[Brisbane Roar FC|Brisbane Roar]]|clubnat=AUS|latest=v. {{fb|TPE}}, 10 June 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=MF|name=Remiyan Muthuccumaru|age={{Birth date and age|2007|04|09|df=y}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[FC Emmen]]|clubnat=NED|latest=v. {{fb|TPE}}, 10 June 2025}}
{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=FW|name=Shenal Sandesh|age={{Birth date and age|2002|7|25|df=y}}|caps=3|goals=0|club=[[Up Country Lions SC|Up Country Lions]]|clubnat=SRI|latest=v. {{fb|YEM}}, 16 November 2024}}
{{nat fs break}}
;Notes
*<sup>INJ</sup> Withdrew due to injury
*<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad / standby
*<sup>RET</sup> Retired from the national team
*<sup>SUS</sup> Serving suspension
*<sup>WD</sup> Withdrew due to non-injury issue
{{nat fs end}}

{| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;"
|-
!colspan=9|[[AFC Challenge Cup]]
! style="width:1% ;" rowspan="16"|
!colspan=6|AFC Challenge Cup qualification
|-

! width=120px| Result

! width=30px| L

! width=30px| W
! width=30px| D*
! width=30px| L

! width=30px| GA
|- style="background:silver;"
|{{flagicon|Bangladesh|2006}} [[2006 AFC Challenge Cup|2006]]||'''Runners-up'''||'''2nd'''||'''6'''||'''4'''||'''1'''||'''2'''||'''7'''||'''2'''||colspan=6|''No qualifying round''
|-
|{{flagicon|India|2008}} [[2008 AFC Challenge Cup|2008]]||Group stage||7th||3||0||0||3||1||9||3
||2||1||0||14||4
|-
|style="border: 3px solid red"|{{flagicon|Sri Lanka|2010}} [[2010 AFC Challenge Cup|2010]]||Group stage||5th||3||1||0||2||4||7||3
||2||1||0||9||4
|-
|{{flagicon|Nepal|2012}} [[2012 AFC Challenge Cup|2012]]||colspan=8 rowspan=2|''Did not qualify''||3
||0||1||2||0||5
|-
|{{flagicon|Maldives|2014}} [[2014 AFC Challenge Cup|2014]]||3
||1||0||2||5||5
|-
!Total||3/5||Runners-up||12||5||1||7||12||18||12||5||3||4||28||18
|}
* In 2011 and 2015 The AFC Challenge Cup acted as the qualification for the Asian Cup.
* The AFC Challenge Cup was cancelled by the AFC.

=== AFC Solidarity Cup ===

|-
! width=150px| Year
! width=150px| Result

! width=40px| L
! width=40px| GF
! width=40px| GA
|-
|{{flagicon|Malaysia|2016}} [[2016 AFC Solidarity Cup|2016]]||Group stage||'''6th'''||3||0||1||2||2||5
|}

== Head-to-head record ==
''Last update was against [[Bhutan national football team|Bhutan]] on 08 June 2026.''<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.eloratings.net/Sri_Lanka.htm|title=Sri Lanka matches, ratings and points exchanged|work=eloratings.net|access-date=6 February 2017|archive-date=16 February 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170216031247/http://www.eloratings.net/Sri_Lanka.htm|url-status=live}}</ref>

==Honours==
<!-- DO NOT ADD UNOFFICIAL OR FRIENDLY COMPETITIONS IN THE SUMMARY TABLE FOR OFFICIAL HONOURS ONLY -->
===Continental===
* '''[[AFC Challenge Cup]]'''
** {{silver2}} Runners-up (1): [[2006 AFC Challenge Cup|2006]]

===Regional===
* '''[[SAFF Championship]]'''
** {{gold1}} '''Champions (1)''': [[1995 SAARC Gold Cup|1995]]
** {{silver2}} Runners-up (1): [[1993 SAARC Gold Cup|1993]]
* '''[[Football at the South Asian Games|South Asian Games]]'''
** {{bronze3}} Bronze Medal (2): [[Football at the 1993 South Asian Games|1993]], [[Football at the 1995 South Asian Games|1995]]

===Summary===
Only official honours are included, according to ''FIFA statutes'' (competitions organized/recognized by [[FIFA]] or an affiliated confederation).
